title: Global Rules
subtitle: Universal rules for all languages and all projects

title: Adapter Layer
subtitle: Adapter is the data exchange hub — the only layer that knows all others
rules: All UI↔Core communication flows through Adapter
Adapter transforms data between representations — Core domain ↔ UI view model
Adapter receives UI events, validates them, dispatches to Core
Adapter reads Core results, maps to UI-ready structs, stores in AdapterState_sta
One Adapter module per major view or feature area
AdapterState_sta contains only UI-facing data — not domain objects
AdapterState_sta is persisted by Gateway on shutdown — see persistent-state.md
UI reads exclusively from AdapterState_sta — never from Core directly
AdapterState_sta is initialized from Gateway on startup
View model types are tagged `_adp` — they are Adapter-layer types
View models are flat, serializable structs — no domain logic
Domain objects (`_core`) never reach the UI — always mapped first
banned: Business logic in Adapter — delegate to Core
Direct UI↔Core communication — Adapter is the mandatory intermediary
Direct UI↔Gateway communication — Adapter mediates all state access
Adapter importing platform-specific APIs — use PAL through Core
axioms: Adapter is the ONLY layer that imports from all other layers
Adapter holds AdapterState_sta — the ViewModel state for the UI

title: Commit Early — Clean Files First
subtitle: Commit every error-free file immediately — never let a clean queue grow
rules: When pre-commit checks pass for a file, commit it immediately
Never batch clean files waiting for other files to be fixed
The only uncommitted files should be those that still have errors
Each commit should contain only related changes — do not lump unrelated clean files
Commit messages describe what changed — not "batch commit" or "fix everything"
banned: Holding back clean files to "commit everything at once"
Commit messages like "batch update" or "fix all files" — each commit has a purpose

title: Cross-Language Consistency
subtitle: Same patterns everywhere — one pattern learned = works in all languages
axioms: Same patterns in Python, JavaScript, C++, Rust, Kotlin
One pattern learned = works everywhere
Syntax differs, structure identical

title: Data-Driven UI — The Paradigm
subtitle: UI is a reactive projection of data. Adapter owns the data. Components are pure structure.
rules: Think of every token as a runtime-controllable data endpoint
The default value in the global file is just the initial state — Adapter can override it
Components must never know WHERE data comes from — they just read globals
Values flow one way: state files → globals → components
Control flows one way: Adapter → globals (via set)
Components never push values — they bind and react
axioms: This is not a naming system — it is a data-driven architecture
Every token is a data source the Adapter can manipulate at runtime
UI contains zero logic and zero values — it projects whatever the globals say

title: Diagrams: Mermaid Only
subtitle: Text-based, version-controlled, AI-readable diagrams
banned: ASCII art diagrams
External diagram tools (draw.io, Lucidchart) for docs
Image-only diagrams (no source)
axioms: ALL diagrams in Mermaid format — no exceptions
Mermaid renders natively in GitHub, GitLab, VS Code, Obsidian
Text-based = version controlled, diffable, AI-readable

title: Edge Types — The Graph's Connection Dictionary
subtitle: A stereotype names a node. An edge type names a connection. Together they define the full typed graph.
rules: If a connection is not in the table, it is not allowed
The table is the single source of truth for valid edges
banned: Unnamed or ad-hoc connections between modules
Inventing new edge types for roles that already have one
Connections justified by "it works" — if the table says no, it is no
axioms: Every connection between nodes has a type — the edge stereotype
Edge types are universal — same names in every project, every language
A connection without a type is an architecture violation — it cannot be validated

title: Error Flow
subtitle: Classify -> match exhaustively -> recover or report — never crash silently
rules: Transient errors retry with backoff before escalating to SystemError path
UserErrors show actionable feedback — app stays in valid state
SystemErrors disable the affected feature — keep the app running
Bugs capture full context, invoke crash reporter, then exit cleanly
Validation catches errors at boundaries BEFORE they enter the system — error flow handles what validation missed
When a third-party exception reaches your code, classify it: retryable? → Transient. User's fault? → UserError. Infrastructure down? → SystemError. Should never happen? → Bug
Every subsystem (feature, background task, connection) runs inside its own error boundary
A failed subsystem is stopped and reported — it does not crash the app
The app is the supervisor — it keeps running and shows which subsystems are available
Subsystems expose a state: Running / Degraded / Failed — UI reflects this state
banned: Wildcard `_ => {}` or `default: {}` error arm that does nothing
`catch(e) {}` empty catch block
`catch(e) { log(e) }` — logging without recovery is silent swallow
Showing stack traces, internal paths, or raw error types to the user
`unwrap()`/`expect()` in application code outside tests
`throw new Error("something failed")` without a typed discriminated error
Single catch-all error type — variants must be distinguishable for recovery
axioms: Every error has a class — class determines the recovery strategy
Match exhaustively — no wildcard arm that silently discards errors
User-facing message at Adapter boundary — never a stack trace, never silence

title: User-Facing and MCP Error Messages
subtitle: Translate internal errors to human-readable strings at the Adapter boundary
rules: Prefix MCP error responses with `ERROR:`, `WARN:`, or `OK:` so callers can parse intent
Include what failed + what to do next — not just the exception message
`ERROR:` blocks the action (fix required); `WARN:` informs but does not block

title: File Size Limits
subtitle: Size follows encapsulation — when a file has one job, it stays small naturally
sections: The Mother/Child Cascade — The Only Correct Split Pattern
rules: After splitting, update all imports and references before moving on
"Approaching" means within 20% of the limit — plan the split before hitting it
Only code lines are counted — comments, doc-comments, blank lines, and string literals are free
Slint and Rust share the same limits — declarative markup and heavily-documented Rust both benefit from comment exclusion
Soft limit = warning, start planning the split
Hard limit = stop, split before writing a single new line
README, spec, and documentation files are exempt — content drives length
"Count before you write" is the habit — not a suggestion
A split always produces a folder + a mother file — never just a new sibling in the same directory
The mother file re-exports everything the old single file exposed — callers change nothing
Children are named after their single responsibility, not after the feature they came from
The folder name matches the original file name — `feature.py` becomes `feature/`
banned: Adding to a file that is already at its limit
"I'll split it later" — split at the trigger, not when it becomes a crisis
Moving code to a sibling file as a "split" — that is relocation, not decomposition
Compressing, removing whitespace, or condensing code to stay under the line limit — the limit is a design signal, not a quota to game
Asking to raise or adjust the limit — the correct response to hitting the limit is always to split
Treating the split as a penalty — modular structure is the preferred design, the limit enforces what good architecture already demands
Creating a flat cluster of siblings with no mother — there is always a single composition point
axioms: One file, one encapsulated module — if the file is growing, it has taken on a second job
Size limits exist because AI loses context above ~250 code lines — not for style reasons
Only code lines count — comments, doc-comments (/// //! /* */), blank lines, and pure string literals are excluded
The line limit is the hard constraint — even a well-encapsulated single-concern module MUST be split when it reaches the limit, because AI cannot reliably edit what it cannot hold in context
Before adding code to any existing file, count its lines — if near the limit, split the module first
A split is NOT moving code sideways — it creates a mother/child folder cascade (see uiux/mother-child.md)
The split IS the solution — modular structure is the goal, not a workaround for hitting the limit

title: Graph-Position Paradigm
subtitle: Every edit happens at a node. The node defines what you may touch.
sections: Why This Produces 80% Fewer Overwrite Errors
The Three Properties of a Node

title: Graph-Position Rules and Relationships
subtitle: Position determines authority — the edit boundary is the graph, not intuition
sections: Adding New Rules Under This Paradigm
banned: Editing a file because it "looks related" — related is read-only
Refactoring nodes you visited for context
Expanding scope silently — boundary conflicts are user decisions, not AI decisions
axioms: You may only edit files reachable from your current node via requires/feeds edges
Reading a file for context does NOT grant edit authority over that file
If a fix requires editing outside your boundary, stop and surface the boundary conflict

title: Graph Traversal as Session Protocol
subtitle: A session is a traversal — start at a node, expand only along declared edges

title: Index System
subtitle: Auto-generated index.yaml as single source of truth for codebase navigation
rules: ALWAYS verify index at session start (step 0 in startup checklist)
NEVER edit index.yaml manually — always regenerate
Pre-commit hook updates automatically on commit
CI fails if index doesn't match regenerated version
Submodules have their own index.yaml
Index is deterministic: same input = same output
axioms: Projects use auto-generated `index.yaml` as single source of truth
Index maps domains → files → sections (no content, only addresses)
Each repo has its own index.yaml (submodules not indexed by parent)

title: Project Initialization
subtitle: Run once — when proj/ does not exist or user says "initialize project"
rules: Build scripts, index generators, and one-off CLI tools belong in `tools/`
All proj/ files are created in one session — do not leave initialization partial
Ask the user before creating proj/UIUX — confirm it is a GUI project
proj/PROJECT.Current.phase must be "1" after initialization
banned: Build scripts or print-based CLI scripts outside `tools/`
Creating proj/ files from assumptions — always read the source doc or ask the user
Leaving initialization partial — either complete all steps or none
axioms: This sequence runs ONCE per project — not every session (that is startup.md)
Do not skip steps — each step feeds the next
After completion, run the full startup.md checklist to verify

title: Install Architecture — Locked
subtitle: Packages install from GitHub — never from local paths
banned: `path = "..."` dependencies in Cargo.toml (creates build-time dependency on local path)
Hardcoded local paths in Cargo.toml, build.rs, or any installed binary
`cargo install --path .` from a local D:\ or C:\ path for production use
axioms: No runtime or install-time dependency on any local drive path (D:, C:\REPO, etc.)
All packages install from GitHub via `pip install git+https://...`
After install, the package is 100% self-contained in site-packages
Moving or deleting the source repo must NOT affect any running MCP server

title: Know Before You Change
subtitle: 90% understanding, 10% changing — the 90/10 rule
axioms: 90% of refactoring is UNDERSTANDING — 10% is changing
Initialize context — read all related files first
Map dependencies, exports, imports before touching code
If you don't understand it, you can't safely change it

title: Language in Code
subtitle: All code and documentation is in English — localize UI strings, never source code
rules: All code, identifiers, comments, and documentation must be written in English
Only ASCII characters are permitted in source code — no unicode in identifiers, strings, or comments
UI strings start as English literals — extract to localization files only when i18n is required
Variable names, function names, file names, and commit messages are always English
All rule files, project files, and repository content must be written in English
AI-human conversation may be in any language — all repo content must be English
banned: Non-ASCII characters in source code identifiers or comments
Native-language strings hardcoded in logic layers — use English keys, localize at the boundary
Mixing languages in the same file or module

title: Module Tree
subtitle: One encapsulated module = one file. "Nested modules" means a folder of files — never nested code in one file.
sections: Why File Size Matters for AI
rules: "Nested modules" = a parent folder containing child files — not nested code inside one file
A module may be 50 lines or 200 lines — size follows naturally from encapsulation
When a module spawns sub-modules, it becomes a folder; its own code moves to an index/root file
The working limit for reliable AI edits is ~200 lines per file
A file that causes AI context loss will produce bugs — split it before that happens
Each ES module export that stands alone is its own file
Named re-exports belong in an index.js — not mixed with implementation
Every named Slint component lives in its own `.slint` file
App-level wiring (routing, modal management) is the only code allowed in the root component file
The parent file after a split is a composer — it imports and arranges, adds no new logic
Sub-modules are named after what they do, not what they contain
A split always follows mother-child — the parent file is a compositor, children are stateless
banned: Multiple independent modules in one file regardless of line count
A "central" file that grows as the project grows — central files are architectural debt
Interpreting "nested module" as adding nested classes or sections inside one file
Barrel files that also contain implementation code
Growing a central `.slint` file — split on the first sign of a second independent component
A flat cluster of sibling files with no compositor — there is always one mother
axioms: One module = one file — the file boundary IS the encapsulation boundary
Every level of nesting in the UI tree = a separate file — no exceptions
A file must be independently comprehensible — if it requires scrolling to understand, it is too large
In JS/HTML/Slint: splitting is not style — it is what makes the code work and what lets AI reason about it
In Slint, 200 lines per file is a practical hard limit — AI loses the property graph above that

title: Mother-Tree Architecture
subtitle: A system always has an origin from which edges emanate. That origin owns state. Everything else is stateless.
rules: Mother passes state down — children never fetch their own
Children emit events up — Mother decides what happens
Siblings never share edges — all coordination routes through Mother
When a child grows too large, it becomes a sub-mother with its own children
banned: Children querying global state, stores, or context directly
Children importing from siblings — horizontal edges break the tree
Extending capability by making Mother or an existing child larger
axioms: **Root Rule** — Every system has exactly one origin (Mother) from which all edges emanate
**State Rule** — Only Mother owns state — children are stateless transforms
**Growth Rule** — Complexity grows outward (new children), never inward (deeper monolith)
If the edges are correct, the system is sound — regardless of what the nodes contain
If the edges are wrong, the system is broken — regardless of how good the nodes are

title: Type Naming — Layer Tag Suffix
subtitle: Every public type carries its layer tag — `BuilderAdapter_adp`, `CoreState_sta`, `AppConfig_cfg`
rules: Suffix is appended to the PascalCase type name — `TypeName_tag`
`grep _adp` finds every Adapter type across all files — the tag is the search key
`_sta` and `_cfg` override the layer tag — state and config structs use their own suffix regardless of layer
When in doubt where a type belongs — pick the layer, apply the tag, move it to the matching folder
If a type spans two layers, move it to `_x` (shared)
banned: Public types without a layer tag suffix
Tag and folder disagreeing — `_core` type in `src/adapter/` is a placement error
axioms: All public types have a layer tag suffix — the tag identifies where the type lives and what it does
Tag and folder always agree — `_adp` lives in `src/adapter/`, `_gtw` lives in `src/gateway/`

title: Flat Code
subtitle: Nesting measures control-flow complexity — not brace depth
rules: Early returns to reduce nesting — guard clauses at top
Extract helpers for complex logic — name the intent
If/else on same level, not nested
banned: Exceeding language-specific nesting limit (see table below)
axioms: Flat is better than nested — all languages, no exceptions
Nesting = control-flow depth, NOT total brace count

title: Parallel State Over Pipeline
subtitle: Uafhængige operationer køres parallelt via state machines — aldrig sekventielt
rules: Kør IKKE sekventiel pipeline når operationerne er uafhængige af hinanden
Brug skifteregister med parallelle state machines — én per operation
Hver operations state persisteres i database — ikke i hukommelsen
Hver operation kører simultant med de øvrige
Crash-recovery sker via database-state — operationen genoptages, ikke genstarter
State transitions er intern puls — ingen ekstern watchdog nødvendig
Hvis operationerne kan defineres uafhængigt af hinanden → de KAN køres parallelt → de SKAL køres parallelt
banned: Sekventiel pipeline hvor operationer ikke har data-afhængighed af hinanden
State i hukommelse for operationer der skal overleve crash

title: Persistent AI Memory
subtitle: RAG + FIXES = AI's persistent memory across sessions

title: Global Quick Reference
subtitle: Universal rules for all languages and all projects — scanners enforce these automatically
sections: Stereotypes — Fixed Names for Fixed Roles

title: Read Before Write
subtitle: Never modify files without reading them first
axioms: NEVER write to a file you haven't read in this session
Read the ENTIRE file, not just a snippet
Understand what exists before changing anything

title: Rules Repo — AI Scope Constraints
subtitle: When working in D:/REPO/Rules — content only, never infrastructure
sections: INFRASTRUCTURE — AI must NOT change without explicit instruction
When asked to "fix" something in this repo
banned: Changing how packages are installed (`pip install -e .` vs `pip install .`)
Changing the install target (site-packages location, user vs system)
Modifying `pyproject.toml` package structure or entry points
Modifying MCP server startup, transport, or configuration
Changing `claude_desktop_config.json` MCP server definitions
Refactoring scanner architecture (how languages are loaded, dispatched)
Adding new dependencies to `pyproject.toml` without explicit request
"Improving" the flow because it seems suboptimal — the flow is intentional
axioms: This rule applies ONLY to the Rules repo (D:/REPO/Rules-dev) — user projects follow their own language-specific init.md which IS authorized to set up build infrastructure
This repo has two distinct concerns — CONTENT and INFRASTRUCTURE
AI is authorized to change CONTENT — AI is NOT authorized to change INFRASTRUCTURE
If unsure which category an action falls into — ASK before acting

title: Secrets Location
subtitle: Central secrets, never copied into projects
rules: Use directly from ~/.env/ — never copy secrets into projects
Reference via symlink or env var, not duplication
banned: Copying .env files into project folders
Secrets in git repos (even gitignored copies)

title: Mandatory Startup Checklist
subtitle: Do this BEFORE answering the user — every session
rules: All project files live in `proj/` — never in the project root
Validate that proj/TODO phase matches proj/PROJECT Current.phase
If proj/ does not exist → follow global/initialize.md — do not improvise
axioms: Step 0 is mandatory — it installs the scan chain: Edit/Write → PostToolUse hook → violations visible immediately + pre-commit blocks ERROR commits
Step 7 is mandatory for any session involving UI, CSS, or source files — oversized files must be split before new code is added

title: Stereotypes — The Graph's Type System
subtitle: A stereotype is a fixed name for a fixed role. Look it up. Never invent.
sections: Why This Is a Graph Concept
rules: Stereotype names the container (folder). Suffix tag names the content (type). Both are required.
banned: Inventing new names for roles that already have a stereotype
Using synonyms — `helpers`, `utils`, `common`, `misc`, `lib`, `services`, `managers`
`helpers` — has no role; split into named children by what they actually do
`utils` — same problem; every "util" belongs in a real module
`common` — use `shared` with `_x` tag
`lib` — use the layer name
`services` — use `adapter` or `gateway` depending on IO involvement
`managers` — a manager is a mother that does not know it; make it explicit
`misc` — if it has no role, it has no place
`infra` — use `gateway` for IO, `pal` for platform abstraction
axioms: Every folder, module, and file role has a canonical name — the stereotype
Stereotypes are universal — same name in every project, every language, every layer
Naming a node is not a design decision — it is a dictionary lookup

title: Technical Debt Markers
subtitle: No debt markers in committed code — fix it or file a ticket
banned: `TODO` comments in committed code — create a ticket instead
`FIXME` comments — fix before committing
`HACK` or `WORKAROUND` comments — refactor instead
`NOCOMMIT` — must never reach a commit
`XXX` markers — use a ticket

title: Tools and Scripts
subtitle: Tools are dependencies you configure — scripts are code you own
sections: 1. Shebang — file declares its own runtime
2. Bin entries — project registers commands with bound runtime
3. Package.json scripts — explicit runner per task
rules: Tools are dependencies — scripts are owned code. The distinction determines where they live and how they are managed.
Scripts belong in `tools/` — not scattered in project root or `src/`
Tool configuration lives in project root — one config file per tool
Bun is the default runtime for all owned JS/TS code
Node is only accepted for framework tooling that requires it
When both runtimes exist in a project, the boundary is structural — each part declares its runtime in config (`package.json` engine, script shebang, or runner config)
Owned scripts always use Bun — even in projects where framework tooling uses Node
Every owned script must have a shebang declaring its runtime
Use bin entries to expose project scripts as named commands
Package.json scripts must use explicit runner prefix (`bun`, `node`, or tool name) — never bare `ts` files without runner
Scripts follow the same naming and module conventions as project code
One script, one job — no multi-purpose script files
banned: Scripts outside `tools/` (except `package.json` scripts entries that call tools)
Tool source code copied into the project — install it as a dependency
Running owned scripts on Node when Bun can run them
Adding a TypeScript build step when Bun runs .ts directly
Implicit runtime choice — if both runtimes exist, every entry point must declare which one
Bare script paths in package.json without explicit runner (ambiguous to AI and humans)

title: Topology Profiles
subtitle: Same 6 layers, different activation — the project type determines which layers exist
sections: Why Each Layer Appears or Not
UI — present when there is a rendering surface
Adapter — present when there is live state between a UI and Core
PAL — present when the app runs on multiple platforms
Gateway — present when the app does IO
MCP Embedded (`--mcp` flag in a desktop/mobile/TUI app)
MCP Stdio (rmcp, FastMCP — subprocess launched by AI client)
Desktop app with MCP (profile: desktop + mcp-embedded)
rules: PROJECT file declares the active profile — e.g. `topology: desktop` or `topology: mcp-standalone`
Scanner only checks import rules between layers that exist in the project
If something presents an interface to a user (human or AI) → UI layer exists
If only one UI surface exists AND it is request-response → no Adapter
If multiple surfaces share state (GUI + MCP, GUI + API) → Adapter is mandatory
If the app targets exactly one platform with no plan to port → skip PAL
If the app targets browser → browser IS the platform, Web APIs are direct
A type belongs in shared only if it is imported by 2+ layers
If a type is only used within one layer, it belongs in that layer
Adding MCP to a GUI app requires zero changes to Core, PAL, or Gateway logic
MCP tools read from AdapterState_sta — same observable state as GUI
MCP tool names map to Adapter event names — they are not independent identifiers
Tool handlers are still `_ui` — they define the AI's interface
Tools are thin — validate input, call Core or Gateway, return result
Auth and transport are `_gtw` — IO infrastructure
mcp-stdio and mcp-standalone have identical layer topology — only Gateway transport differs
mcp-stdio tools follow the same rules as mcp-standalone tools
Pick the profile that matches your app's actual needs — not aspirational needs
Start minimal — add layers when the need arises, not before
banned: `utils/`, `helpers/`, `common/` as shared — shared holds types and constants, not logic
Functions in shared — shared holds types and constants, not logic
MCP tools calling Core directly — must route through Adapter events
MCP tools with logic not available to GUI — same capabilities, different surface
Business logic in tool handlers — delegate to Core modules
Tool handlers doing direct IO (disk, network) — delegate to Gateway
axioms: The topology from global/topology.md is always the reference — profiles activate a subset
A layer is either fully present or entirely absent — no partial layers, no empty folders
The project type determines which layers are active — decided once at project init
Import rules apply only between active layers — absent layers have no folders, no files, no imports
Adapter exists because two surfaces share live state — not because the app is "complex"
MCP tools are `_ui` — parallel to GUI, same layer, same import rules
MCP tools fire Adapter events — same `on_save`, `on_close` as GUI buttons
MCP transport (stdio) is `_gtw` — IO infrastructure, not UI
No Adapter — tools call Core directly or delegate to Gateway
No PAL — single deployment target
No GUI — MCP tools are the only surface

title: Application Topology
subtitle: 6-layer hexagonal MVVM — every file has a home, every import has a direction
rules: Each type's suffix tag matches its folder (e.g. `_adp` lives in `src/adapter/`)
Folder name maps directly to suffix tag — no ambiguity
Gateway is the only layer that touches external IO (disk, network, processes)
Adapter is the only layer that imports from all other layers
A type tagged `_adp` lives in `src/adapter/` — tag and folder always agree
All mutables, literals, and constants live in `src/state/` — no magic numbers or hardcoded values in other layers
Adapter is the sole exception — it owns its own state as the data exchange hub
Config structs (`_cfg`) and state structs (`_sta`) both live in `src/state/`
GUI and MCP are both `_ui` — alternative rendering surfaces for the same Adapter state
`--mcp` flag switches the UI surface from GUI to MCP — Core, Gateway, PAL are identical in both modes
UI ↔ Adapter (events up, computed props down)
Adapter → Core (dispatch); Core → Adapter (results/reads)
Core → PAL (platform operations from business logic)
Adapter → Gateway (state read/write)
Gateway → PAL (disk/network IO via platform abstraction)
PAL → Platform APIs (iOS, Android, Windows, Linux — all platform targets are PAL implementations)
Shared (`_x`) may be imported by any layer
`_sta` and `_cfg` types follow their host layer's import rules
A `_core` file importing a `_adp` type is always a placement error — move the logic up
New type → pick folder → apply matching tag → done
If a type spans two layers, split it or move it to `_x`
Tests live in `tests/` mirroring `src/` — test types use `_test` tag
Never scan code you don't own — vendor/third-party is always excluded
Metadata folders (`proj/`, `doc/`, `man/`) skip code checks but get placement validation
Nested folders inside `src/` are always scanned — `src/docs/` is code, not metadata
Mother passes state down as props/arguments — children never fetch, query, or derive their own state
Children emit events up — mother decides what happens next
Siblings never communicate directly — all coordination routes through their shared mother
Children have no knowledge of each other — they are independently understandable files
Apply this pattern recursively — every subtree has exactly one mother
banned: Circular imports between layers
UI importing Core directly — all communication goes through Adapter
Core importing UI, Adapter, or Gateway
Types living outside their designated folder
Magic numbers, hardcoded strings, or mutable state in `_ui`, `_core`, `_pal`, `_gtw`
Literals scattered across layers — centralize in `src/state/`
Core → Adapter, Core → UI, Core → Gateway (direct)
UI → Core (must go through Adapter)
PAL → Core, PAL → Adapter, PAL → UI, PAL → Gateway
`_ui` file importing a `_core` type — UI must not know Core exists
`_ui` file importing a `_pal` type — UI must not know platform exists
`_ui` file importing a `_gtw` type — UI must not know IO exists
`_core` file importing a `_adp` type — Core must not know Adapter exists
`_core` file importing a `_ui` type — Core must not know UI exists
`_core` file importing a `_gtw` type — Core must not know IO exists
`_pal` file importing a `_core` type — PAL must not know domain exists
`_pal` file importing a `_adp` type — PAL must not know Adapter exists
`_pal` file importing a `_ui` type — PAL must not know UI exists
`_pal` file importing a `_gtw` type — PAL must not know Gateway exists
`_gtw` file importing a `_adp` type — Gateway must not know Adapter exists
`_gtw` file importing a `_ui` type — Gateway must not know UI exists
`utils/`, `helpers/`, `misc/` folders — every file belongs to a layer
Adapter logic in Core or PAL
Code files (`.rs`, `.slint`) in `proj/`, `doc/`, `man/` — these are metadata
Running code quality checks on vendor/third-party code
Reporting issues in generated/build output
Mother files with many `fn` definitions — extract to child modules
Child files with `static` or state-owning constructs — state belongs in mother
Children that own state shared with siblings — route through mother
Children that reach outside their subtree for state — mother passes it down
axioms: This topology is non-negotiable — it is the foundation all other rules build on.
No project may deviate from this structure — not partially, not temporarily, not "for simplicity".
If a rule seems to conflict with topology, topology wins.
All projects follow this 6-layer folder topology — no exceptions
Import direction is one-way — lower layers never import higher layers
At every level there is exactly **one owner** of state and layout for that scope — the "mother"
All other modules at that level are **stateless children** — they receive what they need, emit events up

title: Validation Over Abstraction
subtitle: Never hide complexity behind abstractions — expose and check
rules: Install rulestools in every project — scan on every commit, watch on every edit
Fix all errors before committing — warnings inform, errors block
axioms: Never hide complexity behind abstractions
Use validation layers that EXPOSE and CHECK
Each layer catches what the previous missed

title: Modern Language Versions
subtitle: Not outdated, not bleeding edge — the middle path
rules: Use highest version all modern browsers/runtimes support
AI knows latest LTS best — use it
banned: Legacy syntax for old browser support (unless explicit requirement)
Bleeding edge proposals not yet stable

subtitle: Pull-based documentation — AI asks when it needs, zero overhead when it knows

axioms: OPS never writes source code — output is project files only
OPS is a dialogue, not a monologue — every output is a proposal, not a decision
The human is the final authority on all project decisions
OPS refines iteratively — one question, one proposal, one confirmation at a time

title: Persona & Agent Prompt Engineering
subtitle: Rules for writing persona files and agent prompts that work reliably with Claude. Source: Anthropic prompt engineering best practices (claude.ai/docs).
sections: Agentic Persona Rules (from Anthropic agentic systems guidance)
How Persona Files in This Repo Work
rules: Provide context/motivation for constraints — not just "never X" but "never X because Y"
Use numbered steps for ordered sequences — Claude follows them precisely
Tell Claude what to do, not only what not to do — positive framing first
Put long context (documents, state) before instructions — improves response quality up to 30%
Wrap examples in `<example>` tags (multiple in `<examples>`) — separates them from instructions
Role section = one sentence — focus, not a paragraph
Constraints section explains WHY each limit exists — Claude understands and generalizes
Examples section uses `<example>` tags — 3–5 examples for best results
"You are [Name], [one-line description of what this AI does]."
The role sentence establishes tone, expertise, and behavioral baseline immediately
Name the persona — a named persona is more consistent than an unnamed role
Each hard constraint states its reason — one sentence is enough
Use "never" and "always" sparingly — reserve for genuinely non-negotiable constraints
Use git for state tracking — git log is the record of what has been done
Tests before commits — write tests first, commit only when they pass
Incremental progress — advance steadily on a few things at a time, not everything at once
Save state before context window fills — write progress to files, commit, then continue fresh
Make independent tool calls in parallel — sequential only when one depends on another
Every persona file must have a `requires:` list in frontmatter — these are the rule files
The persona body uses XML tags for all major sections
RULE/VITAL/BANNED markers in the body are enforceable — they also appear in register.jsonl
banned: Writing a persona as a flat wall of text with no XML structure
Overengineering — no abstractions, helpers, or features beyond what was asked
Hard-coding to pass tests — implement the real logic, not the minimal case
Speculating about code before reading it — read first, answer second
axioms: A persona file is a Claude system prompt — it must follow Anthropic's prompting guidelines
Structure content with XML tags — Claude parses tagged sections unambiguously
State the role in one clear sentence at the top of the system prompt
Confirm before irreversible actions — destructive ops, force-push, dropping data, external posts
Investigate before answering — never speculate about files not read, code not opened

title: Gateway Layer
subtitle: The I/O boundary — all disk, network, process, and database access goes here
rules: All IO returns `Result<T, AppError_x>` — Gateway never panics on IO failure
No business logic in Gateway — IO marshalling only

title: Gateway IO
subtitle: Gateway is the only layer that touches disk, network, and processes — everything else is pure
rules: Gateway delegates all actual IO to PAL — no direct syscalls, no `std::fs` outside Gateway
One Gateway module per IO concern — `RuntimeGateway_gtw`, `PublishGateway_gtw`, `McpGateway_gtw`
Path resolution always goes through PAL — never hardcode `~/.config` or platform paths
Gateway structs hold a PAL reference — IO calls go through it, not through `std` directly
Each Gateway method does one IO operation — no compound multi-step methods that mix concerns
banned: `std::fs::read`, `std::fs::write`, `reqwest::get` outside Gateway
`unwrap()` on any IO operation — surface all errors as `Result`
Gateway modules containing business logic — IO marshalling only
`_gtw` file importing a `_adp` type — Gateway must not know Adapter exists
`_gtw` file importing a `_ui` type — Gateway must not know UI exists
axioms: Gateway is the ONLY layer that performs IO — file read/write, network, subprocess, clipboard
All IO returns `Result<T, AppError_x>` — Gateway never panics on IO failure

title: Gateway Lifecycle
subtitle: Gateway is initialized first and shut down last — it owns every layer's state on disk
rules: Gateway returns fully parsed `_cfg` and `_sta` structs to the caller — layers receive, not fetch
Gateway collects all `_sta` registrations at init time — no runtime state discovery
If a state file does not exist, Gateway returns the default `_sta` — never an error
Partial state corruption → log warning, use default — never abort the whole app
banned: Layers fetching their own config or state from disk — Gateway hands it to them
Writing state mid-session from outside Gateway — flush via Gateway only
`_gtw` file importing a `_adp` type — Gateway must not know Adapter exists
`_gtw` file importing a `_ui` type — Gateway must not know UI exists
axioms: Gateway::init runs BEFORE Core, Adapter, and UI — it is the boot entry point
Gateway::shutdown runs AFTER ui.run() returns — all _sta structs are written to disk here

title: Adapter Layer
subtitle: The data exchange hub — owns viewmodel state, routes events, translates types

title: Adapter Event Flow
subtitle: Receive UI event → validate input → dispatch to Core → map result → push complete state to UI
rules: Event handler steps in order: validate input shape → dispatch to Core → map result to _adp → push state
Input shape validation belongs in Adapter — Core trusts its inputs and enforces business rules
All `ui.on_*()` registrations happen before `ui.run()` — never registered lazily or conditionally
Adapter::init() receives `ui`, `core`, and `state` — wires them together, returns self
banned: Pushing partial state updates — always push the whole `AdapterState_sta` after a change
Event handlers that read from `ui.get_*()` to decide what to do — state lives in Adapter, not UI
Business logic in event handlers — one call to Core, no conditions around it
axioms: Adapter is the ONLY layer that registers `ui.on_*()` event listeners — in `Adapter::init()`
After every event handler, push the COMPLETE updated `AdapterState_sta` to UI — not just changed fields

title: Adapter ViewModel
subtitle: Domain objects stop at the Adapter boundary — UI only sees flat, UI-ready structs
rules: One `AdapterState_sta` per Adapter module — it contains exactly what that view needs
Status flags (`is_loading`, `error_message`, `is_empty`) are always explicit in `AdapterState_sta`
`AdapterState_sta` is initialized from Gateway on startup and persisted on shutdown
View model types are flat, serializable structs tagged `_adp` — no methods, no domain logic
Map at the Adapter boundary — Core returns `_core`, Adapter converts to `_adp` immediately
Mapping functions live in `src/adapter/mapping.rs` — one place for all `_core` → `_adp` conversions
Dates, prices, counts — format to display strings in the mapping, not in the UI component
banned: `_core` types in `AdapterState_sta` or passed to `ui.set_*()`
Optional fields that hide loading or error state — make all states explicit
Computed display values (formatted strings, derived booleans) in `_core` types — compute in Adapter
axioms: Domain objects (`_core`) never reach the UI — Adapter maps them to `_adp` view models first
`AdapterState_sta` is the complete description of what the UI renders right now

title: Core Layer
subtitle: Pure business logic — no I/O, no UI, no platform dependencies
rules: Core imports zero UI, Adapter, Gateway, or platform code
Testable with `cargo test` alone — no mocking of platform or I/O

title: Core Design
subtitle: Core is pure business logic — same input, same output, no side effects, no platform
rules: Platform operations (file paths, clipboard, appearance) go through PAL — Core defines the need, PAL provides it
One module per domain concern — one file, one responsibility, one `_core` type family
Core returns `Result<T, AppError_x>` — it never panics, never crashes silently
`_core` types are the domain vocabulary — `Field_core`, `Collection_core`, `Schema_core`
Validation of business rules belongs here — validation of input shape belongs in Adapter
banned: `use slint`, `use gtk`, `use winit`, `use tokio`, `use std::fs`, `use reqwest` in Core
Core knowing about screen names, view models, or UI structure
Core calling Gateway or Adapter — it only calls PAL when platform behaviour is needed
`_core` file importing a `_adp` type — Core must not know Adapter exists
`_core` file importing a `_ui` type — Core must not know UI exists
`_core` file importing a `_gtw` type — Core must not know IO exists
axioms: Core imports ZERO UI, Adapter, Gateway, or platform code — it is fully isolated
Core functions are pure where possible — same input always produces the same output

title: PAL Layer (Platform Abstraction Layer)
subtitle: One trait per concern, one implementation per platform — only layer with unsafe and FFI
rules: `#[cfg(target_os)]` lives ONLY inside PAL implementations
Only layer allowed to use `unsafe` code and OS-specific crates
Adding a new platform = one new `_pal` implementation — zero changes elsewhere

title: PAL Design
subtitle: One trait per concern, one implementation per platform — platform code lives nowhere else
rules: One trait interface per concern — `FilePal_pal`, `WindowPal_pal`, `AppearancePal_pal`
Core and Gateway receive PAL as `Arc<dyn SomePal_pal>` — never import concrete implementations
Each target platform has one PAL implementation struct tagged `_pal`
PAL implementations are the ONLY place that imports OS-specific crates (`winapi`, `objc2`, `libc`)
Adding a new platform = adding one new `_pal` implementation struct — zero changes to Core or Gateway
iOS is a PAL implementation (`IosFilePal_pal`, `IosWindowPal_pal`) — not a separate architecture layer
Android is a PAL implementation — same pattern
Platform selection (`#[cfg]`) happens in `main.rs` — constructs the right PAL, injects via trait
After construction, the rest of the app sees only `Arc<dyn FilePal_pal>` — no platform conditionals
banned: `#[cfg(target_os)]` outside `src/pal/`
Business logic or data transformation inside PAL — it executes, not decides
`_pal` file importing a `_core` type — PAL must not know domain exists
`_pal` file importing a `_adp` type — PAL must not know Adapter exists
`_pal` file importing a `_ui` type — PAL must not know UI exists
`_pal` file importing a `_gtw` type — PAL must not know Gateway exists
axioms: PAL is stateless — it delegates to platform APIs, returns a result, holds nothing
Platform-specific code (`#[cfg(target_os = ...)]`) lives ONLY inside PAL implementations

title: PAL Traits
subtitle: The contracts between Core/Gateway and the platform — implement once per target
rules: Every PAL trait method returns `Result<T, AppError_x>` — platform calls can always fail
Trait methods are narrow — one operation, no overloaded concerns
PAL traits live in `src/pal/traits.rs` — implementations in `src/pal/<platform>.rs`
Core and Gateway only call methods on these traits — never call OS APIs directly
`watch_appearance` runs in its own thread — sends changes via channel, Adapter posts to UI via `invoke_from_event_loop` (see slint/threading.md)
Unit tests for Core and Gateway use `TestFilePal_pal` — no real disk, no platform dependency
Test doubles live in `src/pal/` alongside real implementations

title: UI/UX Rules
subtitle: Perfect UI per platform — structure, state flow, and platform behaviour
sections: Paradigm — how to structure UI code
rules: One file per concern — component, token group, state type, feature area each get their own file
axioms: UI components contain **zero literal values** — every color, size, spacing is a named token
UI components are **stateless** — state flows in from Adapter, events flow out
All apps **persist state** on close and restore on launch — users return to where they left off

title: About and Shortcuts — Desktop Platforms
subtitle: Use the platform-native mechanism — GNOME, macOS, Windows, KDE

title: About — Mobile Platforms (Android + iOS)
subtitle: Dedicated About screen reached from navigation drawer or Settings

title: About and Shortcuts — Web / PWA
subtitle: Modal dialog or `/about` route, shortcuts overlay via `?` key
rules: `?` key opens shortcuts overlay — do not require Ctrl/Cmd modifier on web
About page must contain: app name, version, author, license, source link

title: New App Checklist
subtitle: Verify before shipping — all platform UX requirements

title: UI Component Rules
subtitle: One file, one component, one job — small by default
rules: One component per file — file name matches component name
Compose via small components, never via deep nesting
Components are stateless by default — receive state in, emit events out
No business logic inside components — delegate to Adapter
HomeScreen shows the home — not home AND sidebar AND header
Extract a sub-component whenever a section can be named independently
Formatting, conditional rendering, computed labels → computed property in Adapter — not in the component
If you must scroll to understand a component → split it now
One screen component ≤ 3 levels of nesting visible at a glance
File name = component name, PascalCase (UserCard.tsx, UserCard.kt, user_card.py)
Screen/page entry points: `NameScreen` or `NamePage` — e.g. `HomeScreen`, `SettingsPage`
Reusable components: descriptive noun — `UserCard`, `ActionButton`, `LoadingSpinner`
Private sub-components may live in the same file only if they are ≤ 20 lines and used nowhere else
banned: Components that render multiple independent UI concerns
Inline conditional logic deeper than one level — extract to sub-component
Side effects inside render / composable / template body
God components that own multiple screens' worth of markup
axioms: Every UI component has exactly one responsibility
Count lines before adding to a component file — at 80 lines plan the split, at 100 lines stop and split first

title: Context Menus
subtitle: Right-click menus, primary menu, and minimum required items
rules: Right-click (secondary click) MUST show context menu if relevant actions exist
Context menu MUST also be accessible via `Shift+F10` or `Menu` key
Long press on touch = right-click equivalent
Menus should contain 3–12 items
Label commands with verbs: "Open", "Copy", "Delete"
Group related items with separators
Items must have access keys (underlined letter)
Every app MUST include in the primary menu:
banned: Nested submenus — flat structure only
Close/Quit in the primary menu

title: Drag and Drop
subtitle: File-to-terminal path insertion, file tree, and external sources
rules: Dragging a file from file tree into terminal inserts the file path at cursor
Drop targets MUST show visual feedback when drag hovers
GTK4: Use `:drop(active)` CSS pseudoclass
Invalid drop targets show "not allowed" cursor
Show drag icon representing what is being dragged
Every drag-and-drop action MUST have a keyboard/menu alternative

title: UI File Structure
subtitle: Folders by feature — each folder is a UI area, each file is one component
rules: One folder per UI area or screen
One component per file — never dump multiple components in one file
Folder name matches the area or screen it represents
Sub-components used only in one area live in that area's folder
Shared components used in 2+ areas live in `src/ui/shared/`
Screen file is the entry point — it composes from sub-components in its folder
When a component is needed in a second area → move it to `shared/`
Depth max 2: `src/ui/<area>/<file>` — no deeper nesting of folders
Toolkit template files (`.ui`, `.blueprint`) co-locate with their code file — same folder
File name = component name — no suffix like `UserCardComponent` or `UserCardView`
banned: Flat `ui/` folder with all components at the same level
Type-based folders (`src/ui/buttons/`, `src/ui/cards/`) — use feature folders
Multiple independent components in one file
Copying a component into a second folder — promote to shared instead
axioms: UI files are organised in folders per feature/screen area — not by type

title: GTK4 — Rust + gtk-rs
subtitle: glib::clone!, composite templates, weak refs, signal handlers
rules: `glib::clone!` for closures capturing Rc/Arc — prevents strong-reference cycles in signal handlers
Composite templates for complex widgets — keep widget tree in .ui XML, not in Rust code
Weak references in signal handlers — never hold strong refs from signals
Actions for keyboard shortcuts — use `gio::SimpleAction`, not manual key handlers
State lives in a central `AppState_sta` struct — widgets only render, never own state
Widget callbacks emit events up — they do not mutate state directly
State mutations happen in the Adapter, results flow back to widgets via property bindings
One `.ui` file per widget — keeps the Rust file focused on behaviour only
Template children via `#[template_child]` — no manual widget lookup by ID
axioms: Widgets are stateless — all state lives in the Adapter layer, widgets only display

title: Help, About, and Shortcuts
subtitle: Every GUI app must have About, a license, and a shortcuts overview — delivered natively per platform
rules: About is reached from the primary menu (hamburger) → "About [AppName]"
Shortcuts overview is reached via `Ctrl+?` and from the primary menu
Help is reached via `F1` — minimum: opens shortcuts window or a help view
About must contain: app name, version, short description, author name, website, license
Group shortcuts by function area, not by key
List only app-specific shortcuts — skip universal ones unless they are non-obvious
Update shortcuts window whenever a shortcut is added or removed
banned: Shipping without About, license, or shortcuts overview
Custom About dialog on platforms that provide a standard one (GNOME, macOS, KDE)
axioms: Every GUI app ships with an About dialog, a license, and a keyboard shortcuts overview
Use the platform-native mechanism — never a custom dialog where a standard one exists

title: Issue Reporter Integration
subtitle: Separate process for crash survival — shared across all apps
rules: Issue Reporter runs as its own process, NOT embedded in the host app
Launch as detached process — must survive host crash

title: Keyboard Shortcuts
subtitle: Standard shortcuts and navigation — GNOME HIG compliant
rules: Terminal emulators (VTE) intercept Ctrl+C/V — use Shift modifier
Use `Ctrl+Letter` as primary pattern
Use `Ctrl+Shift+Letter` for reverse/extended functions
Make shortcuts mnemonic (Ctrl+F = Find, Ctrl+S = Save)
App-specific shortcuts use `Ctrl+Shift+` prefix
Only assign shortcuts to commonly-used actions
Always use the standard shortcut if the function matches
Menu items show shortcut labels automatically via GTK/Qt actions
Tooltips on buttons mention shortcuts: `"Zen Mode (Ctrl+Shift+Z)"`
Context menus expose actions for right-click users
Shortcuts dialog (`Ctrl+?`) lists all available shortcuts
Header bar menu includes "Keyboard Shortcuts" entry
banned: `Super+*` — all Super combinations (GNOME Shell)
`Alt+Tab` — window switching
`Alt+F4` — close window
`Alt+F6/F7/F8` — window management
`Ctrl+Alt+Delete` — power off
`PrintScreen` variants — screenshots
Three/four-finger gestures — system gestures
`Alt+Letter` for shortcuts — conflicts with access keys
`Super` — reserved for GNOME Shell
axioms: Use GTK Actions + Accelerators (`app.set_accels_for_action`), NOT `EventControllerKey`, which won't fire while VTE has focus.

title: Android Navigation — Kotlin + Jetpack Compose
subtitle: No menubar on Android — Bottom Navigation + Top App Bar overflow menu
rules: Top App Bar overflow menu (three-dot) contains: Settings, Help, About, Report Issue
Settings opens a dedicated Settings screen via NavController — not a dialog
About is a full-screen composable or AlertDialog
Back navigation is handled by the system — do NOT add explicit back buttons to top-level screens
Use NavController + NavHost for all navigation — no manual Fragment transactions
Switch to NavigationDrawer when destination count exceeds 5 — do not squeeze BottomNav
About screen shows: app icon, name, version, description, website link, license link
Website and license are tappable — not plain text
banned: Traditional menubar widget on Android
BottomNavigationBar with more than 5 items
Mixing Fragment transactions with NavController
Opening Settings as a Dialog — it must be a full screen
axioms: Android has no menubar — use BottomNavigation for primary nav, overflow menu for secondary actions
Bottom Navigation max 5 items — use Navigation Drawer if more destinations are needed

title: GNOME Menus — Rust + Slint
subtitle: No native menubar on GNOME — use HeaderBar with hamburger PopupMenu in Slint
rules: Hamburger menu (≡) in top-right of HeaderBar contains: Shortcuts, Preferences, Help, About
About is a Slint dialog (no GTK AdwAboutDialog available from pure Rust/Slint)
Shortcuts window is a Slint PopupWindow or Dialog — opened by Ctrl+?
Register Ctrl+? and F1 as Slint key handlers — no muda needed on Linux
banned: Native GTK menubar — GNOME HIG explicitly forbids traditional menubars
Putting Quit in the hamburger menu — Ctrl+Q is sufficient, Quit belongs nowhere in GNOME UI
axioms: GNOME does NOT have a window menubar — never add one, it violates HIG
Primary actions go in the HeaderBar — secondary actions in the hamburger PopupMenu

title: iOS Navigation — SwiftUI
subtitle: No menubar on iOS — TabView for primary nav, toolbar items for secondary actions
rules: Secondary actions go in .toolbar { ToolbarItem } — not in a custom overlay
About is presented as a sheet or pushed NavigationLink
Settings belong in a dedicated Settings screen pushed via NavigationLink
Use .contextMenu for long-press actions on list items
Back navigation is automatic via NavigationStack — no custom back button on standard screens
Use `@AppStorage` for simple persistent settings — no manual UserDefaults
Present settings as a sheet (modal) from toolbar — not a tab item
About shows: app icon, name, version, description, website link, license link
Use `Link` for website and license — tappable, opens in Safari
Destructive context menu actions must use `role: .destructive`
banned: Custom menubar widget on iOS
TabView with more than 5 tabs without restructuring
Putting app settings inside an alert or sheet — use a full NavigationLink screen
axioms: iOS has no menubar — use TabView for top-level navigation, NavigationStack for hierarchy
TabView max 5 items — use "More" pattern or restructure if more destinations needed

title: KDE Menus — Rust + Slint
subtitle: Hamburger menu by default — optional global menubar export via DBus for Plasma
sections: Optional: Global Menu via muda (Plasma only)
rules: Default to hamburger menu in HeaderBar — same pattern as GNOME
If exporting to global menu: use `muda` with the `dbus` feature on Linux
Shortcuts use `Ctrl+?`, Preferences use `Ctrl+,`, Quit uses `Ctrl+Q`
About is a Slint dialog — same component as GNOME (reuse AboutDialog.slint)
Shortcuts window is a Slint dialog — same component as GNOME (reuse ShortcutsWindow.slint)
Always fall back to hamburger if global menu DBus service is unavailable
Never require global menu — treat it as progressive enhancement
banned: Assuming global menu is always present — many KDE users disable it
axioms: KDE Plasma supports a global menubar (exported via DBus) but does not require it

title: macOS Menus — Rust + Slint
subtitle: NSMenu via muda — the menubar lives at the top of the screen, always
rules: Preferences uses `Cmd+,` — not `Ctrl+,`
Quit uses `Cmd+Q` — PredefinedMenuItem::quit() sets this automatically
Shortcuts window uses `Cmd+?` (Shift+Cmd+/)
Menu is installed before `ui.run()` — after the window handle is available
Always set name, version, authors, website, license, copyright in AboutMetadata
issue_url optional but recommended — shows as "Report a Bug" link on macOS
banned: Ctrl+Q or Ctrl+, on macOS — use Cmd modifiers
Custom drawn menubar — NSMenu is mandatory on macOS
axioms: macOS apps MUST have a native NSMenu — Slint renders no menubar on macOS
The Apple menu (leftmost) is system-managed — add About and Quit there via PredefinedMenuItem

title: Qt6 Menus — C++ / QML
subtitle: QMenuBar on desktop — platform-native delivery on macOS and Windows automatically
rules: File > Preferences on Windows/Linux — Application menu on macOS (Qt handles this via QAction::ApplicationSpecificRole)
Use QKeySequence::StandardKey for all shortcuts — never hardcode platform-specific keys
QML: use MenuBar { Menu { Action {} } } — not custom-drawn overlays
About dialog must show: name, version, description, website, license
Always use `StandardKey.*` enum values — Qt maps them per platform automatically
`setMenuRole` / Action role routes items to the macOS Application menu without #ifdef
About shows: name, version, description, website link, license link
Website and license are clickable links via `onLinkActivated`
banned: Custom drawn menubar widget replacing QMenuBar / MenuBar
Hardcoded key strings (e.g. "Ctrl+,") when QKeySequence::Preferences exists
Separate About actions for macOS and non-macOS — set the role and Qt routes it
axioms: Use QMenuBar attached to QMainWindow — never a custom toolbar widget replacing the menu
Qt6 automatically places About and Preferences in the macOS Application menu — let it

title: Menus in Rust + Slint
subtitle: Native menus via `muda` on macOS/Windows — Slint-native on Linux
rules: `muda` is the crate for native menus in Rust — works with Slint's winit backend
Menu structure (items, labels, shortcuts) is identical across platforms — only delivery differs
All menu events are handled in the main Rust event loop, not in Slint callbacks
About, Shortcuts, and Help are required in every app — see help-about.md
banned: Custom drawn menubar on macOS or Windows — use native
Blocking the Slint event loop with menu callbacks
axioms: Use platform-native menus where the platform has them — never fake a menubar with Slint widgets on macOS or Windows

title: Windows Menus — Rust + Slint
subtitle: Win32 native menu via muda — attached to the window frame
rules: Use `Ctrl+,` for Preferences (Windows convention, not Cmd)
Use `Ctrl+Q` for Quit (Windows convention)
Use `Ctrl+?` for Shortcuts (Ctrl+Shift+/)
Menu is attached to the HWND after Slint creates the window
About dialog is a custom ContentDialog-equivalent — no native standard exists on Windows
About dialog shows: name, version, description, author, website, license — all fields
Website and license are clickable links, not plain text

axioms: Windows apps should have a native menu bar attached to the window — not a custom Slint toolbar

title: Mother-Child — Jetpack Compose Implementation
subtitle: AppShell owns state and layout — children accept `Modifier` from mother
rules: Mother passes `Modifier.width()` — child never sets its own width
Child accepts `modifier: Modifier = Modifier` as first parameter — applies it to root composable
`when` block in mother selects active view — children do not know about each other

title: Mother-Child — React/SolidJS Implementation
subtitle: AppShell owns state and layout — children are pure functions of props
rules: Mother sets `style` variables for layout — children fill their slot with `100%` or `flex: 1`
Children accept a props interface — never import from sibling components or context

title: Mother-Child — SwiftUI Implementation
subtitle: Mother owns `@StateObject` — children observe via `@Binding` or `@ObservedObject`
rules: Mother view owns `@StateObject` — single source of truth
Children receive `@Binding` or `@ObservedObject` — never own state
`@EnvironmentObject` for deep injection (theme, config) — not for component state
Mother is the only `View` that creates the `ObservableObject`
banned: `@State` in child views for data that belongs to mother
`@StateObject` in children — they observe, never own
Children fetching data directly — mother provides all inputs

title: Mother-Child UI Composition
subtitle: One mother owns everything — children know nothing except what mother gives them
sections: Why This Works for AI Development
The Topology Rule Applied to UI
rules: Mother passes state down to each child as props — children never fetch or derive their own
Children emit events up to mother — mother decides what to do with them
All styling that affects layout (size, position, flex, grid, padding between sections) lives in mother only
A child may style only its own internal presentation (font weight, icon color, inner padding)
Every child file is independently understandable — no hidden state, no global dependencies
Mother is the **only** place where `if activeView === 'A'` logic lives
NavBar does not know that ViewA exists — it only emits `navigate(viewId)`
EditorPanel does not know what selected the item — it only receives it
Views may only be imported by Mother — never by another View
A component used in 2+ views must be promoted to `widgets/` — never copied
Overlays (modals, dialogs, toasts) are children of mother — not of the view that triggered them
Overlay visibility is state owned by mother — children emit `show-overlay()`, mother toggles it
When a view grows beyond ~10 children, check `widgets/` before creating more view-specific children
Children use `width: 100%`, `flex: 1`, `fill-available`, or the equivalent — they fill their slot
This pattern mirrors the Adapter → UI relationship — Adapter is "mother" for the whole UI layer
Apply this pattern recursively — if a child has sub-children, the child becomes mother for its subtree
At each level there is exactly one owner of state and layout for that subtree
banned: Child components querying global state, stores, or context directly
Child components importing from sibling components — siblings never know each other
Hardcoded width, height, or min/max-size in a child component
Children sharing state with each other — all shared state routes through mother
Mother delegating state ownership to a child and re-reading it back
A View importing another View — extract shared components to `widgets/` instead
Copying a component into multiple views — promote to widgets/
An overlay owned by or embedded inside a child view
`width: 240px` inside `NavBar.tsx` — that measurement belongs to mother
`position: absolute` inside a child that fixes itself to a screen edge
axioms: Every UI has exactly one mother — the root component that owns all state and all layout
Children are stateless modules — they render what they receive, nothing more
Children have no hardcoded sizes — mother controls all dimensions and positions
Views are direct children of mother — they see only what mother gives them
Widgets are the shared component library — generic, stateless, importable by anyone

title: Pointer and Touch
subtitle: Click behavior, hover rules, touch interaction
rules: Single click to activate — GNOME does not use double-click for primary actions
Click targets must be large enough for comfortable use
Hover MUST NOT reveal essential actions or information (inaccessible on touch)
Tooltips are acceptable (supplementary info only)
Long press = right-click (context menu)
Two-finger gestures available for app use (pinch zoom, two-finger scroll)
banned: Simultaneous multi-button presses
Referencing specific devices in UI text (no "click", use "select"/"activate")
Hover-only menus or buttons

rules: Public pages must be server-rendered with Blade in the first version
Views must not contain publication or authorization logic
Views only display already-decided data — all logic resolved before the view
Markdown must be converted to HTML through a dedicated service
Public HTML output must be sanitized before rendering
Theme behavior must be domain-configurable
banned: Business rules in Blade (state checks, locale fallback, routing decisions)
Complex conditionals in templates — resolve in controller/ViewModel
Rendering unsanitized AI-generated HTML directly to public output
Article files written to disk as primary source of truth — render from database

title: Eloquent Models
subtitle: Models = data shape + relations + simple scopes — nothing more
rules: Models must not contain complex domain workflows
Model = data shape + relations + casts + simple scopes
Business flow belongs in Action/Service classes, not models
Use Eloquent directly — no repository abstraction without concrete need
banned: Fat models with business workflow methods
`Article::publishNowAndInvalidateCachesAndNotify()` — split into Actions
Repository pattern unless there is a concrete need (e.g., swappable data source)
Repository interfaces for standard database access

title: Laravel Install

rules: Pick Livewire OR Inertia — not both for the same feature

title: Laravel proj/ Templates
subtitle: Standard proj/ files for Laravel projects — create all in one session
rules: Ask user before creating UIUX — confirm it is a GUI project
All proj/ files created in one session
proj/PROJECT.Current.phase must be "1" after initialization
axioms: Fill in `[bracketed]` sections — do not leave placeholders

title: Laravel Project Initialization
subtitle: Entry point — choose stack, install, create proj/ files
rules: All steps are completed in one session — do not leave initialization partial
Stack choice (step 1) determines which install path to follow
banned: Skipping stack choice — decide Blade or Svelte before installing anything
Installing all stack options blindly — pick what the project needs
axioms: Run this ONCE per project — not every session (that is startup.md)
Do not skip steps — each step feeds the next

title: Migrations & Schema
subtitle: All schema changes through migrations — immutable revisions, append-only events
rules: All schema changes must be implemented through Laravel migrations
Article revisions must be immutable — no updates, only new rows
Event logs must be append-only where practical
Domain + locale + slug must be unique (composite unique constraint)
banned: Schema changes outside migrations
Updating revision rows — create new revision instead
Deleting event log entries in normal operation

title: Laravel Quick Reference
subtitle: All rules at a glance

title: Routing & Middleware
subtitle: Route model binding where it helps, middleware for cross-cutting concerns
rules: Route model binding — but only where it doesn't hide important domain logic
API authentication must be handled by middleware
External systems may never write directly to the database
Separate API and Web route groups with appropriate middleware stacks
Domain resolution must be configuration-driven, never folder-driven
banned: Business logic in middleware — only cross-cutting concerns
Domain-as-folder architecture

title: Services & Dependency Injection
subtitle: DI over facades — services are technical helpers, not business logic
rules: Dependency injection over facades in domain logic
Services are technical helpers — not business orchestration
No facades in core domain logic when DI is clearer
banned: Overuse of facades in core domain logic
Large static helper classes
Random `helpers.php` with unstructured global functions
"Utility" classes with 40 mixed methods
Direct use of `env()` outside config files
No repository abstraction without a concrete need

title: Stack 1 — Laravel + Blade
subtitle: Pure PHP stack — server-rendered views for internal tools and admin backends
rules: Blade stack is for projects where UI complexity does not justify a frontend framework
Views render already-decided data — all logic resolved before the template
Use ViewModels when view data preparation becomes complex
banned: Business logic in Blade templates
Building user-facing products with this stack — use Stack 2
Heavy JavaScript in Blade projects — if you need it, switch to Stack 2

title: Stack 2 — Laravel + Inertia + Svelte
subtitle: Svelte owns the UI, Laravel owns the server, Inertia is the bridge
rules: All user-facing products use this stack
Svelte owns all UI rendering — PHP never generates user-facing HTML in this stack
Laravel owns all server logic — data, auth, validation, jobs, queues
Inertia is the only bridge between Svelte and Laravel — no separate REST API for the frontend
Server-side logic stays in PHP — do not duplicate validation or business rules in JS
JS/TS runtime (Node, Bun, Deno) is a project choice — none are excluded
User-facing products should target PWA where applicable
banned: PHP-rendered HTML for user-facing pages — Svelte handles all UI
Building a separate REST API for Svelte when Inertia handles the data flow
Duplicating server validation in the frontend

title: Stacks
subtitle: Two stacks, never mixed — project type determines the stack
rules: Stack 1 is for server-side tools and internal dashboards — not user-facing products
Stack 2 is for all user-facing products — PWA, websites, apps
A project uses exactly one stack — never mix Blade UI and Svelte UI in the same project
banned: Mixing Stack 1 and Stack 2 in the same project

title: Explicit State Flow
subtitle: State is always explicit — transitions are explicit — no hidden magic
rules: State is always an explicit enum value — never inferred from timestamps
State transitions must be handled in application logic (Actions), never inferred from queries
Public visibility is determined by state + date rule — no hidden magic
Event logs should be append-only where practical
banned: Implicit publication — "if `published_at` is set then maybe it's live"
State inferred only from timestamps or nullable columns
State transitions hidden in views or queries
Business rules in Blade templates that determine publication state

title: Testing
subtitle: Feature tests for HTTP, unit tests for Actions — real database always
rules: Feature tests for HTTP endpoints — full request/response cycle
Unit tests for Action/Service classes
Real database with `RefreshDatabase` trait — not mocks
Factories for test data setup
Test state transitions explicitly
banned: Mocking Eloquent models or database layer
Tests that depend on execution order

title: Laravel Topology
subtitle: 6-layer mapping for Laravel projects — both Blade and Svelte stacks
rules: Controllers are adapter — they transform, not decide
Actions are core — business logic lives here, not in controllers or models
Eloquent models are gateway — they touch the database
Laravel itself is the platform — no separate PAL layer needed

title: Validation
subtitle: FormRequest for all inbound data — no raw input in business logic
rules: All inbound API/form payloads must use FormRequest validation
Controllers may not access raw input arrays when a validated request object exists
No untyped arrays passed deeply through the system — use DTOs or validated data objects
banned: `$request->all()` or `$request->input()` when `$request->validated()` exists
Raw `$_GET`, `$_POST` anywhere
Passing unvalidated arrays deep into service/action layers
