title: SID Architecture — PROTOTYPE
subtitle: Design philosophy for data-driven systems built for conversation-based development

title: Code Free of Mutables — Principle 02
subtitle: Source code contains no mutable values. All constants, labels, thresholds, defaults, colors, paths and URLs are SIDs. Coverage is categorical.
rules: All strings, numbers with domain meaning, colors, fonts, URLs, paths, defaults are SIDs
The data file is one canonical source — code accesses it only through the resolver layer
Resolver function is named consistently across the codebase (`resolve()`, `sym()`, `$()`)
Validation at build: every SID in code exists as a record, every record is referenced, types match
banned: Literal values in function bodies that carry domain meaning
"Too small to deserve a SID" — padding of 8px is a SID
Defaults in declarations (`let timeout = 5000`)
Strings in comparisons (`if theme == "dark"`)
Enum values defined in code that represent domain states
axioms: Principle 02 is categorical — everything addressable is a SID, no exceptions
Data records live in files in the repo and are read at build time

title: Data-Driven Runtime — Principle 03
subtitle: After compilation, the parts of the system that should be changeable must be determined by SID records in a live datastore, not by code or build-time data.
rules: Coverage is selective — only what must vary at runtime lives in the datastore
The engine contains mechanics and infrastructure, no domain content for what is changeable
The datastore contains SID records for what the system must change without rebuild
UI always tends toward full coverage — UI changes more often than business logic
banned: Build-time import (`import symbols from './symbols.json'`) as "data-driven" — that is Principle 02
Hardcoded enum domains for what should be runtime-configurable
Expressions in data (`condition`, `if`, `when`, `formula`) — that is pseudocode
axioms: The engine knows *how*; the datastore says *what*
Data-driven and declarative are different things — this is architectural, not linguistic

title: Environments
subtitle: Compiled environments have an automatic boundary. Interpreted environments require an architectural boundary. The medium often chooses for you.
rules: In compiled environments the compiler automatically draws the boundary between Principle 02 and 03
In interpreted environments the architecture must create the boundary — build a parser/loader that distinguishes sources
The medium draws the boundary naturally — HTML+JS are easy on disk, JSON is easy in database
UI is always Principle 03 regardless of environment

title: Meta-Driven UI
subtitle: The UI is not code that renders data. The UI *is* data interpreted by an engine.
rules: The same engine can drive arbitrarily many UIs by pointing at different databases
A widget exists as a double entity: physical file on disk + record in database
Screen compositions contain only SIDs — no hardcoded strings or values
Granularity decisions are made at the data level without architecture revision
axioms: The engine is precompiled code; the UI is records in database; the parser is the link
The widget catalog (all widget records) is the system's UI API, fully readable as data

title: SID Identity
subtitle: A SID is a 6-character identifier starting with a letter followed by alphanumeric characters. It is the value itself, not a pointer to a value.
rules: Format is 6 characters, first char `[a-zA-Z]`, chars 2-6 `[a-zA-Z0-9]`, case-sensitive
SIDs are purely opaque — no prefix, no type indicator, no scope marker
Generation via simple generator: random string → uniqueness check → return
Codebase + data files + datastore together *are* the registry — no separate authoritative source
Declarations are always SIDs — module-level constants, struct fields, properties, field names, events
Local bindings in functions may have ordinary names if the value is fully derived from SID lookups
UI markup follows stricter rule — no local-binding exception in markup
banned: Literal values in code that carry domain meaning — everything is a SID
Names as identifiers — names are mutable fields on the SID
Local bindings with literal values not derived from SIDs
axioms: The SID *is* the entity — name, type, value, relations are fields added afterwards
All addressable points in the system are SIDs — constants, variables, declarations, widgets, rules

title: Topology as Data
subtitle: Data expresses what *is*. Code expresses what *happens*. Rich structure in data is good. Logic in data is bad.
sections: Canonical Names as a Superpower for AI
rules: Data may be as rich as you want, as long as it stays in the noun category
Canonical names carry topology — AI reads the entire structure as a graph
When the data schema requires something beyond SID + value + named relations, the engine is missing a mechanism
banned: Expressions in data (`condition`, `if`, `when`, `evaluate`, `formula`, `script`, `rule_body`)
Pseudocode disguised as JSON/TOML
axioms: Structure yes, logic no — data is nouns, code is verbs

title: Working in Data
subtitle: About 50% of development work in a mature SID-based system happens in data — not in code.
rules: Value changes never touch code files; structure changes never touch data files
Principle 02 ensures code is clean enough; Principle 03 ensures changes reach the running system
axioms: The data layer is a self-contained workspace — like CSS is for styling, but for *everything* that can be data
The more structure that is explicit in data, the easier it is for AI to reason about the system

title: Phase System — research-first phases with AI-signoff
subtitle: JSON-schema methodology for breaking projects into phases that actually work before they close. Built to compose with [anti-scope-creep-rigging](https://git.lpmintra.com/ai/anti-scope-creep-rigging).
rules: Decisions persist with candidates, answer, rationale, and `lands_in` (where the answer lives)
Every plan step has a testable `pass` criterion — outcome, not implementation
Failed verification blocks done — either fix, or move to a new phase with reasoning in `issues_logged`
Every file in `<n>-<label>/` starts with `p<n>-` prefix
Sub-folder names match a layer (or sub-layer) in `proj/data/current/architecture.json`
Different sub-folders run parallel; same sub-folder is serial by default
banned: Nested `<n.x>-<label>/` folders for x > 0 — sub-phases share the major's folder
Generic `phase.json` filenames — collides across phases, always `p<n>-<slug>.json`
`parts.items[].parallel_with` — v0.3 derives parallelism from folder placement
axioms: One product per phase — a coherent usable experience, not a checklist of components
AI must be able to demonstrate the product (`product.user_can[]` is concrete and AI-executable)
Research before plan — no plan step written on guessed behavior
No phase closes without AI signoff — `ai_verified=true` after a concrete "I did X, Y, Z and it works" statement
User approval still required after AI signoff (separate step, user-only)

title: Folder conventions (v0.3+)
subtitle: Parallelism is **structural**, not declarative. The folder a part-file lives in is its write-domain. Parts in different folders can run concurrently; same-folder parts are serial.
sections: Rule 1 — One folder per major phase, named `<n>-<label>/`
rules: `<label>` after `<n>-` is free — `data`, `setup`, `fundament`, `ink-tools`, `voice-pipeline`. Pick what aids `ls`-scanning.
Orchestrator's `identity.title` is the canonical title; the folder label is navigation.
Every file in `<n>-<label>/` starts with `p<n>-` (orchestrator: `p<n>.json`; parts: `p<n>-<slug>.json`; sub-orchs: `p<n>.5.json`, etc.)
Two parts in different sub-folders can run concurrently — assumed disjoint write-sets
Two parts in the same sub-folder run serially — assumed write-conflict
`research/`, `ops/`, `design/` are commonly added as cross-cutting work-types even though they don't map to a single architecture layer
banned: `<n.x>-<label>/` folders for x > 0 — sub-phases are sub-orchestrators inside the major's folder, not separate folders
Ad-hoc folder names like `cool-stuff/` that don't map to an architecture layer
`phase.json` — collides across phases, gives the filesystem no parallelism affordance
Adding a `parallel_with` exception instead of refactoring the folder structure — the structural property (folder = write-domain) is what makes the system reliable
Folder named after a part-slug (`1-data/p1.5-oauth-fix/p1.5-oauth-fix.json`) — folder names a write-domain, not repeats the slug
Folder that doesn't map to a layer in `architecture.json` — either add the layer (with `$hint`) or rename the folder
Sub-orchestrator inside a sub-folder (`1-data/research/p1.5.json`) — sub-orchestrators are cross-folder coordinators, they live in the root of `<n>-<label>/`
Same-folder parts claimed as parallel because "they won't really conflict" — if you can't refactor into sub-folders, accept they're serial
axioms: Sub-folder names mirror layers (or sub-layers) from your project's `proj/data/current/architecture.json` — couples phase-system to anti-scope-creep-rigging through shared vocabulary

title: Integration with anti-scope-creep-rigging
subtitle: [`ai/anti-scope-creep-rigging`](https://git.lpmintra.com/ai/anti-scope-creep-rigging) and `phase-system` are two halves of the same approach.
rules: Before each plan-step, AI cross-references rigging with four questions
Use the self-check questions in `dsl-guide.json → for_ai_sessions.self_check_*` as the pre-commit filter
Forces deliberate evolution — the rigging doesn't drift; it's revised between phases by explicit decision
banned: Direct edits to `proj/data/` during a phase — always go through the snapshot protocol at close
axioms: Lock `proj/data/` readonly (`chmod -R a-w proj/data/`) — changes go through phase-system snapshots, not direct edits
Architecture evolves only at phase-done via deliberate snapshot — never drift between phases

title: Methodology — what a phase is and how it flows
subtitle: A phase is a unit of work bounded by **one product**. Not a deliverable list — a single coherent thing a user (or AI as a user) can demonstrably use at the end.
rules: Decisions carry candidates (≥2), answer, rationale, and `lands_in` (where the answer is persisted)
Open decisions block plan — resolve via research or defer to a future phase in `issues_logged`
Issues discovered mid-phase that are out of scope go to `issues_logged` with `severity` and `moves_to`
New invariants or anti-patterns discovered go into `anti_creep_check.new_*_discovered` for snapshot at close
Pass criterion is the *outcome*, not the implementation — "tool returns SID for new scene" not "function at file.ts:42"
Planner produces a shelf-ready phase artifact; execution happens in later sessions
Use split-mode when multiple sessions will work concurrently, or when one session spawns parallel sub-agents on independent slices
When in doubt, start single-mode; split when parallelism actually appears as a need
banned: Declaring a phase done because tests pass but the product has never been used
Writing plan steps referencing behavior that was never researched
Phases that produce "a bunch of components" rather than one coherent product
Mid-phase scope creep — new ideas go to `issues_logged`, not into the current plan
Planner session executing plan-steps — that collapses the planner/worker split (exception: trivial phases)
Parts constantly modifying each other's state across folders — that means they're not independent; re-decompose
axioms: One product per phase — if you have multiple products, you have multiple phases
`product.user_can[]` is concrete and AI-demonstrable — if AI can't do it, the product isn't done
Research happens before any plan step is written — no plans on guesses
Every plan step has a testable `pass` — outcome, not implementation
Usage-tests are scenarios AI must be able to perform with the running product — added throughout the phase
Phase cannot close without AI signoff — `ai_verified=true` after a concrete "I did X, Y, Z and it works"
User approval still required after AI signoff — signoff is not approval of own work

title: References syntax — the read-graph (v0.3+, v0.4 excerpts)
subtitle: A part's `references[]` declares what it reads or needs to be aware of. Distinct from folder placement (write-graph), `concurrency.runs_after` (sequential deps), and `anti_creep_check.invariants_touched` (rules-graph).
rules: References is distinct from `runs_after` (sequencing) and from folder placement (write)
Anchor points to a section of the file; it is *advisory*, not programmatically enforced
Excerpt (v0.4) is a pre-distilled quote or summary (~500 chars max) so new sessions get insight without chasing every link
Planner fills `excerpt` once; later sessions get insight for free
When a referenced file changes after the excerpt was written, set `excerpt_stale: true` (paired with change-notification tooling)
External MCP sources prefix the path with `<mcp-name>:` — e.g. `rules-mcp:global/edge-types.md`, `docs-mcp:auth/oauth-flows.md`
Without `references[]`, every new session re-discovers what to read and often misses critical context
Add a reference when you read a file at session-start to ground yourself AND a new session would need to read the same AND (optionally) a change to that file would invalidate your assumptions
Empty `items: []` is honest for greenfield research-only parts
banned: Adding references for every file you happen to read while exploring — reserve for load-bearing knowledge dependencies
Fabricating references to look thorough — empty `items: []` is an honest signal for greenfield parts
Padded references that mask a part with no real knowledge dependencies

title: Session resumption — audit trails and `claude --resume` recovery (v0.4)
subtitle: Every Claude Code session has a JSONL log at `~/.claude/projects/<project-path-encoded>/<session-id>.jsonl`. Phase-system uses this for audit trail and for resuming interrupted sessions instead of starting from scratch.
sections: How a worker claims a part
rules: Session ID equals the JSONL filename (minus `.jsonl`)
The session claiming a part MUST populate all three lock fields — `locked_by_session`, `locked_session_log`, `locked_at`
Clearing all three lock fields releases the lock (done by `close_protocol` at completion)
`lock_policy: 'advisory_24h'` — another session may reclaim a lock that's older than 24h with no status change
When encountering a stale lock, first try `claude --resume <locked_by_session>` — if the previous session was just paused (laptop slept, network hiccup), this picks up cleanly
If resume isn't desired or fails, the new session overwrites `locked_by_session` and `locked_session_log` with its own values
The previous session's JSONL stays — it's still audit trail and can be referenced from `history` entries on the part
banned: Partial lock state — never set only some of the three fields
axioms: Work-in-progress is never lost as long as the JSONL exists — this is the core resilience pattern
The session log is the unit of recoverable work; the part-file is the unit of coordination

title: Application Model
subtitle: CORE + PAL + Adapter + Gateway — stateless components, per-layer persistent state
rules: CORE = pure business logic, no UI, no platform
PAL = platform abstraction layer, stateless
Adapter = data exchange hub, ViewModel, event routing — see adapter-layer.md
Gateway = IO adapter, loads config+state on startup, saves on shutdown
UI = declarative, stateless, MVVM for all except CLI
CSS tokens and design values live in UiConfig_cfg / AdapterState_sta
CLI is the only type without MVVM — it maps args directly to CORE calls
All UI types use declarative rendering from AdapterState_sta
AdapterState_sta = UI view state (selected items, scroll pos, loading flags)
CoreState_sta = domain session state (caches, computed results, active sessions)
GatewayState_sta = IO state (connection pool, retry counts, last-sync timestamps)
All `_sta` structs implement `Persistable_x` — Gateway handles disk IO
UI reacts to changes in AdapterState_sta — observable, not polled
CSS design tokens live in UiConfig_cfg — theme switching = config reload
CORE is input to output — pure functions where possible
CORE reads from CoreState_sta, writes results back to CoreState_sta
PAL is stateless — it delegates to platform, returns result
One PAL interface, multiple implementations (Linux, macOS, Windows, Web)
File I/O, networking, clipboard, notifications — all through PAL
Gateway is initialized first — all other layers receive config+state from it
Gateway uses PAL for all actual IO — no direct syscalls
One Gateway per app — it is the IO boundary
Adapter is the only layer that imports from all other layers
Adapter reads AdapterState_sta, exposes computed properties for UI
Adapter receives UI events, validates input shape, dispatches to CORE
One Adapter module per major view or feature area
UI binds to Adapter properties — no direct state struct access
UI is stateless — all state comes from Adapter
UI sends events to Adapter — never calls CORE directly
MCP server is `_ui` — it is the AI's rendering surface, not a bus or gateway
`--mcp` flag switches the UI surface from GUI to MCP — full stack otherwise identical
banned: Mutable state outside `_sta` structs
UI logic in CORE
Platform calls without PAL
Imperative UI mutation
Disk IO outside Gateway
Import UI frameworks in CORE
Import platform APIs in CORE
Side effects without PAL
Other layers reading/writing files directly
Gateway containing business logic — it only marshals IO
Business logic in Adapter — delegate to CORE
`setState`, `getElementById`, imperative widget updates
Inline business logic in templates/views
MCP server calling Core or Gateway directly — must route through Adapter
axioms: All apps follow this 6-layer hexagonal MVVM model — no exceptions
Per-layer `_sta` structs hold all mutable state — Gateway persists them
No single global state store — state is distributed per layer, coordinated by Gateway

title: Config-Driven Design
subtitle: No hardcoded values — all constants come from `_cfg` structs loaded by Gateway
rules: Every configurable value lives in a `_cfg` struct in `src/state/`
Config structs are passed as parameters — never accessed globally
Gateway loads config from disk on startup, passes it down the call chain
Config structs are immutable after startup — no runtime mutation
Default values are defined in `src/state/`, not scattered in code
All literals (URLs, thresholds, labels, sizes) centralized in `src/state/`
`_cfg` structs are read-only during a session
`_sta` structs are read-write during a session — see persistent-state.md
Config struct names follow `<Layer>Config_cfg` or `<Feature>Config_cfg`
Each layer that needs config gets its own `_cfg` struct
Gateway composes layer configs into a root `AppConfig_cfg`
Gateway reads config file → constructs `AppConfig_cfg` → passes to each layer
Core receives `CoreConfig_cfg` as a parameter — it never calls a config API
Adapter receives `UiConfig_cfg` — it never reads env vars or files
Config files live in `~/.config/<app>/config/`
Gateway discovers config path via PAL — never hardcodes paths
Default config is written by Gateway on first run if config file is absent
banned: Magic numbers in Core, Adapter, UI, or PAL
Hardcoded strings (URLs, paths, thresholds, labels) outside `_cfg` structs
`Config::default()` called outside Gateway — Gateway owns initialization
Global mutable config singletons
Environment variable reads outside Gateway
axioms: Zero hardcoded values in business logic, UI, or adapter code
Zero hardcoded values in UI styling — colors, sizes, spacing, fonts are design tokens (see uiux/tokens.md)
Gateway is the sole entry point for loading configuration

title: Persistent State
subtitle: Per-layer state structs — Gateway handles all disk IO, layers own their state
rules: State structs are tagged `_sta` — this is distinct from the layer tag
`<Layer>State_sta` prefix identifies the owning layer (AdapterState_sta, CoreState_sta)
Gateway loads all state on startup, distributes to layers as parameters
Gateway saves all state on shutdown (or explicit flush)
Layers never call disk IO directly — they hand state back to Gateway
`Persistable_x` trait is the contract between Gateway and state structs
If a value changes while the app runs → it's state (`_sta`)
If a value only changes between runs (via config file edit) → it's config (`_cfg`)
State struct name = `<OwnerLayer>State_sta`
Prefix shows ownership — `grep <Layer>State` finds the owner immediately
`grep _sta` finds all state structs across all layers
Each `_sta` struct implements `Persistable_x`
Gateway collects all `_sta` refs at startup registration — no runtime discovery
Config files in `~/.config/<app>/config/` — read-only during session
State files in `~/.config/<app>/state/` — read on startup, written on shutdown
Gateway discovers paths via PAL — never hardcodes `~/.config`
banned: Global mutable state
Disk access (file read/write) outside Gateway
State embedded in domain objects (`_core` types hold domain data, not session state)
State stored in UI components — UI is stateless, state lives in `_sta` structs
axioms: Gateway is the ONLY layer that reads from or writes to disk for state
Each layer owns exactly one `_sta` struct for its persistent state

title: UI State Flow
subtitle: State in from Adapter, events out to Adapter — components never own domain state
rules: Components are pure renderers — given the same state, they render the same output
The Adapter owns AdapterState_sta — UI reads it, never mutates it
Every loading, error, and empty state must be handled — no silent blanks
Local UI state (focus, hover, open/closed) is the only state a component may own
One-way data flow — state flows down, events flow up
Components declare what they need (typed props/state-in) — never reach up for it
Events carry only the data needed — not the full state object
`ItemViewModel_adp` is a flat, UI-ready struct — no domain objects reach the component
Status flags (`is_loading`, `error`, `is_empty`) are always explicit — never inferred in the component
Every component that shows async data MUST handle: loading, error, empty, and content
Focus, hover, tooltip visibility, animation progress — fine as local state
Open/closed for a dropdown or modal within one component — fine as local state
banned: Fetching data from inside a component
Calling domain/business functions from inside a component
Mutating shared state directly from the UI
Rendering only the happy path and leaving loading/error as blank screen
Local state for anything the Adapter or Core cares about
Derived state re-computed in the component — compute it in the Adapter
axioms: UI components receive state from Adapter — never from Core or Gateway directly
UI components emit events to Adapter — never call Core directly

title: Core State
subtitle: CoreState_sta owns domain session data — caches, active sessions, computed aggregates
rules: `CoreState_sta` holds domain session state — computed results, active selections, caches
Gateway deserializes `CoreState_sta` from disk on startup and passes it to Core as a parameter
Core updates `CoreState_sta` internally — no other layer mutates it directly
Gateway serializes `CoreState_sta` to disk on shutdown via `Persistable_x`
`CoreState_sta` contains only domain types (`_core`, `_x`) — never view models (`_adp`)
`state_key()` matches the filename Gateway writes — `"core"` → `state/core.toml`
`Default` implementation is the first-run state — no separate init logic needed
banned: UI state (scroll, selection, active panel) in `CoreState_sta` — that belongs in `AdapterState_sta`
Config values in `CoreState_sta` — config is `_cfg`, state is `_sta`

title: VCS Rules
subtitle: Version control and repository management rules

title: VCS: Project Repository
subtitle: Every project gets its own repo on Forgejo (git.lpmintra.com) at creation time
sections: What lives in the repo (tracked by git)
rules: Ask the user which org to use if not already defined in project context
Initialize git in the project directory with the Forgejo repo as remote
Create a `.gitignore` that excludes `proj/` from the repository
axioms: When creating a new project, always create a matching repository on Forgejo via VCS MCP
All non-code, non-documentation files live in `proj/` — never in project root
